event: Early hunter-gatherer communities, including ancestors of Baka and other forest peoples, inhabit the equatorial rainforests of present-day Cameroon.
The Bantu expansion is widely thought to have originated in the Grassfields region of western Cameroon, from where Bantu-speaking peoples gradually dispersed across central and southern Africa.
Iron-working and settled agriculture spread across the region, supporting larger sedentary farming communities.
The Sao civilization flourishes around the floodplains near Lake Chad in the far north, leaving distinctive terracotta and bronze artifacts.
Portuguese navigators led by Fernando Po reach the estuary of the Wouri River, naming it Rio dos Camaroes (River of Prawns), the origin of the name Cameroon.
European traders begin commerce along the Cameroonian coast, exchanging goods and increasingly captives, drawing the region into the Atlantic slave trade.
The Bamum kingdom is founded in the Grassfields under the Nshare dynasty, growing into one of the region's most powerful and organized states.
Fulani jihadists under Usman dan Fodio's movement extend the Sokoto Caliphate's influence into northern Cameroon, establishing the Adamawa Emirate.
British explorer Hugh Clapperton and others advance European knowledge of the interior, while Britain pressures coastal chiefs to end the slave trade.
English Baptist missionary Alfred Saker establishes a mission station, later founding the settlement of Victoria (Limbe) in 1858.
Germany signs treaties with Douala chiefs and proclaims the protectorate of Kamerun, formalized during the Berlin Conference dividing Africa.
King Ibrahim Njoya of Bamum develops the original Bamum script, a remarkable indigenous writing system, and reforms his kingdom's administration.
German forces complete the conquest of the interior, suppressing resistance and consolidating colonial control over Kamerun.
France cedes territory to Germany in the Treaty of Fez, enlarging German Kamerun (Neukamerun) in exchange for recognition of French control in Morocco.
Allied British and French forces defeat the German garrison in Kamerun during World War I, ending German colonial rule.
The territory is partitioned into French Cameroun and British Cameroons under League of Nations mandates after the war.
The League of Nations formally confirms the French and British mandates over their respective portions of Cameroon.
The mandates are converted into United Nations trust territories, committing the administering powers to prepare the territories for self-government.
The Union of the Peoples of Cameroon (UPC) is founded, becoming the leading nationalist party demanding independence and reunification.
French authorities ban the UPC after riots, triggering a prolonged and violent independence insurgency.
UPC leader Ruben Um Nyobe is killed by French forces, intensifying the guerrilla war for independence.
French Cameroun gains independence on 1 January as the Republic of Cameroon, with Ahmadou Ahidjo becoming its first president, and the country joins the United Nations.
In UN-supervised plebiscites the Southern Cameroons votes to join the Republic of Cameroon while the Northern Cameroons joins Nigeria, forming the Federal Republic of Cameroon.
Cameroon becomes a founding member of the Organisation of African Unity, the predecessor of the African Union.
Ahidjo merges the political parties into the single Cameroon National Union, establishing a one-party state.
The long-running UPC insurgency is effectively crushed with the capture and execution of guerrilla leader Ernest Ouandie.
A national referendum abolishes the federal structure, creating the unitary United Republic of Cameroon.
President Ahidjo resigns and Prime Minister Paul Biya succeeds him as president.
Biya survives a coup attempt by elements of the Republican Guard and renames the country the Republic of Cameroon.
Multiparty politics is reintroduced after protests, and the Social Democratic Front opposition party is launched in Bamenda.
Cameroon holds its first multiparty presidential election, which Biya wins amid opposition claims of fraud.
Cameroon and Nigeria clash militarily over the oil-rich Bakassi Peninsula, prompting Cameroon to take the dispute to the International Court of Justice.
Cameroon joins the Commonwealth of Nations, reflecting its bilingual British and French colonial heritage.
The International Court of Justice awards the disputed Bakassi Peninsula to Cameroon.
The Chad-Cameroon Petroleum Pipeline, running to the port of Kribi, becomes operational as a major economic project.
Cameroon and Nigeria sign the Greentree Agreement, finalizing Nigeria's withdrawal from the Bakassi Peninsula.
Violent protests over rising prices and a constitutional change removing presidential term limits leave dozens dead.
Cameroon mobilizes its military to combat the Nigerian-based jihadist group Boko Haram, which launches cross-border attacks in the Far North.
Strikes by Anglophone lawyers and teachers over marginalization escalate into a broad protest movement in the English-speaking northwest and southwest regions.
Anglophone separatists symbolically declare the independent state of Ambazonia, igniting an armed conflict with government forces.
Paul Biya wins another term in disputed elections, extending one of the world's longest tenures as a sitting head of state.
Cameroon hosts the delayed Africa Cup of Nations football tournament amid the ongoing Anglophone crisis and security concerns.
Cameroon holds a presidential election with the long-ruling Paul Biya, in his nineties, again a central figure amid persistent Anglophone separatist and Boko Haram insurgencies.
